title: 〜は〜です (topic + copula)
pattern: N は N/Adj です
meaning: X is Y — identifies or describes the topic
explanation: The は particle marks the sentence topic. です is the polite copula 'is/am/are'. This is the most fundamental Japanese sentence structure.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本です。","reading":"これはほんです。","english":"This is a book."},{"japanese":"田中さんは学生です。","reading":"たなかさんはがくせいです。","english":"Tanaka-san is a student."},{"japanese":"私は日本人です。","reading":"わたしはにほんじんです。","english":"I am Japanese."}]

title: 〜は〜じゃありません (negative copula)
pattern: N は N/Adj じゃありません
meaning: X is not Y — polite negative identification
explanation: じゃありません (or ではありません in formal writing) is the polite negative form of です. Used to deny or contradict a statement about the topic.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本じゃありません。","reading":"これはほんじゃありません。","english":"This is not a book."},{"japanese":"私は先生じゃありません。","reading":"わたしはせんせいじゃありません。","english":"I am not a teacher."},{"japanese":"田中さんは学生じゃありません。","reading":"たなかさんはがくせいじゃありません。","english":"Tanaka-san is not a student."}]

title: 〜でした / じゃありませんでした (past copula)
pattern: N/Adj でした / じゃありませんでした
meaning: Was / was not — past tense of the copula
explanation: でした is the polite past affirmative copula. じゃありませんでした is the polite past negative. Used to describe past states or identities.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日は休みでした。","reading":"きのうはやすみでした。","english":"Yesterday was a holiday."},{"japanese":"子供の頃、元気じゃありませんでした。","reading":"こどものころ、げんきじゃありませんでした。","english":"I was not energetic as a child."},{"japanese":"彼は学生でした。","reading":"かれはがくせいでした。","english":"He was a student."}]

title: 〜が (subject marker)
pattern: N が V/Adj
meaning: Marks the grammatical subject, often with focus or new information
explanation: が marks the subject of a sentence and often carries a sense of emphasis or introduces new information. Contrasts with は which marks a known topic.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"猫がいます。","reading":"ねこがいます。","english":"There is a cat."},{"japanese":"雨が降っています。","reading":"あめがふっています。","english":"It is raining."},{"japanese":"誰が来ましたか。","reading":"だれがきましたか。","english":"Who came?"}]

title: 〜を (object marker)
pattern: N を V
meaning: Marks the direct object of a transitive verb
explanation: を marks what the action of the verb is directed at. It is always followed by a transitive verb and cannot be used with intransitive verbs.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"水を飲みます。","reading":"みずをのみます。","english":"I drink water."},{"japanese":"本を読みました。","reading":"ほんをよみました。","english":"I read a book."},{"japanese":"音楽を聴きます。","reading":"おんがくをききます。","english":"I listen to music."}]

title: 〜に (location / time / destination)
pattern: N に V (ある/いる/行く/来る etc.)
meaning: Marks location of existence, point in time, or destination
explanation: に has multiple uses: it marks the location where something exists (with あります/います), a specific point in time, or the destination of movement verbs like 行く and 来る.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"机の上に本があります。","reading":"つくえのうえにほんがあります。","english":"There is a book on the desk."},{"japanese":"三時に来てください。","reading":"さんじにきてください。","english":"Please come at three o'clock."},{"japanese":"学校に行きます。","reading":"がっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school."}]

title: 〜で (location of action / means / reason)
pattern: N で V / N で N
meaning: Marks the place where an action occurs, the means used, or scope
explanation: で marks where an action takes place (contrasting with に which marks existence), the tool or means used to do something, or the scope within which something applies.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"図書館で勉強します。","reading":"としょかんでべんきょうします。","english":"I study at the library."},{"japanese":"バスで学校に行きます。","reading":"バスでがっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school by bus."},{"japanese":"日本語で話してください。","reading":"にほんごではなしてください。","english":"Please speak in Japanese."}]

title: 〜へ (direction)
pattern: N へ V (行く/来る/帰る etc.)
meaning: Marks direction or destination of movement (interchangeable with に for destination)
explanation: へ indicates the direction toward which movement is heading. It emphasizes the direction rather than arrival, and is often interchangeable with に for destination but sounds slightly more literary.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"東京へ行きます。","reading":"とうきょうへいきます。","english":"I am going to Tokyo."},{"japanese":"家へ帰りました。","reading":"いえへかえりました。","english":"I went home."},{"japanese":"日本へようこそ。","reading":"にほんへようこそ。","english":"Welcome to Japan."}]

title: 〜と (and / with / quotation)
pattern: N と N / N と V / V と言う
meaning: Connects nouns exhaustively ('and'), marks a companion ('with'), or introduces a quote
explanation: と connects nouns as an exhaustive list (listing all items, unlike や which is partial). It also marks who an action is done together with, and introduces direct or indirect quotations.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"りんごとオレンジを買いました。","reading":"りんごとオレンジをかいました。","english":"I bought apples and oranges."},{"japanese":"友達と映画を見ました。","reading":"ともだちとえいがをみました。","english":"I watched a movie with a friend."},{"japanese":"彼は「行きます」と言いました。","reading":"かれは「いきます」といいました。","english":"He said 'I will go'."}]

title: 〜の (possessive / noun modifier)
pattern: N の N
meaning: Indicates possession, affiliation, or modifies the following noun
explanation: の links two nouns, showing that the first noun modifies or belongs to the second. It can indicate possession, material, category, or other attributive relationships.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは私の本です。","reading":"これはわたしのほんです。","english":"This is my book."},{"japanese":"日本語の先生はどこですか。","reading":"にほんごのせんせいはどこですか。","english":"Where is the Japanese teacher?"},{"japanese":"東京の大学に行きたいです。","reading":"とうきょうのだいがくにいきたいです。","english":"I want to go to a university in Tokyo."}]

title: 〜も (also / too / even)
pattern: N も V/Adj
meaning: Indicates 'also' or 'too', adding the marked item to what was already stated
explanation: も replaces は, が, or を to mean 'also' or 'too'. When used with negatives it means 'either' (〜もありません = there isn't one either). It can also mean 'even' for emphasis.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"私も日本語を勉強しています。","reading":"わたしもにほんごをべんきょうしています。","english":"I am also studying Japanese."},{"japanese":"コーヒーも紅茶もあります。","reading":"コーヒーもこうちゃもあります。","english":"There is both coffee and tea."},{"japanese":"彼女も来ませんでした。","reading":"かのじょもきませんでした。","english":"She didn't come either."}]

title: 〜か (question particle)
pattern: S か
meaning: Turns a sentence into a yes/no question
explanation: か added at the end of a sentence with rising intonation forms a polite question. In casual speech, か alone can sound blunt, so か with ね or a rising tone is common.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本ですか。","reading":"これはほんですか。","english":"Is this a book?"},{"japanese":"明日来ますか。","reading":"あしたきますか。","english":"Are you coming tomorrow?"},{"japanese":"日本語が話せますか。","reading":"にほんごがはなせますか。","english":"Can you speak Japanese?"}]

title: 〜ね / 〜よ (sentence-final particles)
pattern: S ね / S よ
meaning: ね seeks agreement ('right?'); よ asserts new information ('you know')
explanation: ね is used when seeking confirmation or sharing a feeling with the listener. よ asserts information the speaker believes the listener doesn't know yet. Both soften the directness of a statement.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"いい天気ですね。","reading":"いいてんきですね。","english":"It's nice weather, isn't it?"},{"japanese":"このケーキはおいしいですよ。","reading":"このケーキはおいしいですよ。","english":"This cake is delicious, you know."},{"japanese":"明日は休みですね。","reading":"あしたはやすみですね。","english":"Tomorrow is a holiday, right?"}]

title: Verb ます form (polite non-past)
pattern: V-ます
meaning: Polite present/future affirmative verb form
explanation: The ます form is the standard polite verb ending used in most daily conversation. It can express habitual actions, general truths, or future intentions depending on context.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"毎日学校に行きます。","reading":"まいにちがっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school every day."},{"japanese":"明日映画を見ます。","reading":"あしたえいがをみます。","english":"I will watch a movie tomorrow."},{"japanese":"日本語を話します。","reading":"にほんごをはなします。","english":"I speak Japanese."}]

title: Verb ません form (polite negative)
pattern: V-ません
meaning: Polite present/future negative verb form
explanation: ません is the polite negative non-past ending. It negates the action described by the verb. ませんか is also used to make polite invitations or suggestions.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"お酒を飲みません。","reading":"おさけをのみません。","english":"I don't drink alcohol."},{"japanese":"日曜日は学校に行きません。","reading":"にちようびはがっこうにいきません。","english":"I don't go to school on Sundays."},{"japanese":"分かりません。","reading":"わかりません。","english":"I don't understand."}]

title: Verb ました / ませんでした (polite past)
pattern: V-ました / V-ませんでした
meaning: Polite past affirmative and negative verb forms
explanation: ました is the polite past affirmative. ませんでした is the polite past negative. Both express completed actions or states in the past.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日本を読みました。","reading":"きのうほんをよみました。","english":"I read a book yesterday."},{"japanese":"朝ごはんを食べませんでした。","reading":"あさごはんをたべませんでした。","english":"I didn't eat breakfast."},{"japanese":"映画は面白かったですか。","reading":"えいがはおもしろかったですか。","english":"Was the movie interesting?"}]

title: V て form (te-form)
pattern: V-て / V-で
meaning: Connects actions sequentially or serves as base for many grammatical structures
explanation: The て-form connects two or more actions in sequence. It is also the base for many important constructions: て+います, て+ください, て+もいい, etc. Its form varies by verb group.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"手を洗って、ご飯を食べます。","reading":"てをあらって、ごはんをたべます。","english":"I wash my hands and eat rice."},{"japanese":"ドアを開けて、入ってください。","reading":"ドアをあけて、はいってください。","english":"Please open the door and come in."},{"japanese":"起きて、シャワーを浴びました。","reading":"おきて、シャワーをあびました。","english":"I got up and took a shower."}]

title: 〜ています (ongoing action / current state)
pattern: V-て + います
meaning: Expresses an ongoing action or a resulting state that continues
explanation: て+います has two core meanings: an action currently in progress (I am doing X), or a state that results from a past action and continues (the window is open). Context determines which applies.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今、テレビを見ています。","reading":"いま、テレビをみています。","english":"I am watching TV now."},{"japanese":"窓が開いています。","reading":"まどがあいています。","english":"The window is open."},{"japanese":"彼は東京に住んでいます。","reading":"かれはとうきょうにすんでいます。","english":"He lives in Tokyo."}]

title: い-adjective + です
pattern: Adj-い です
meaning: Polite sentence ending with an い-adjective
explanation: い-adjectives end in い and directly precede nouns or conclude sentences. Adding です makes the sentence polite. The い is part of the adjective, not added—e.g., 高い is already complete.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この映画は面白いです。","reading":"このえいがはおもしろいです。","english":"This movie is interesting."},{"japanese":"今日は寒いですね。","reading":"きょうはさむいですね。","english":"It's cold today, isn't it?"},{"japanese":"そのケーキは甘いです。","reading":"そのケーキはあまいです。","english":"That cake is sweet."}]

title: な-adjective + です
pattern: Adj-な です (drop な before です)
meaning: Polite sentence ending with a な-adjective
explanation: な-adjectives (also called nominal adjectives) require な when directly modifying a noun (静かな部屋) but drop it before です (部屋は静かです). They conjugate differently from い-adjectives.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この公園は静かです。","reading":"このこうえんはしずかです。","english":"This park is quiet."},{"japanese":"彼女はとても親切です。","reading":"かのじょはとてもしんせつです。","english":"She is very kind."},{"japanese":"日本語の勉強は大変ですか。","reading":"にほんごのべんきょうはたいへんですか。","english":"Is studying Japanese hard?"}]

title: い-adjective negative (〜くないです)
pattern: Adj-い → Adj-くないです
meaning: Polite negative form of い-adjectives
explanation: To negate an い-adjective, drop い and add くないです (or くありません in more formal speech). いい becomes よくない, not いくない — this is the one irregular case.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今日はあまり寒くないです。","reading":"きょうはあまりさむくないです。","english":"It's not very cold today."},{"japanese":"この映画は面白くないです。","reading":"このえいがはおもしろくないです。","english":"This movie is not interesting."},{"japanese":"この問題は難しくないです。","reading":"このもんだいはむずかしくないです。","english":"This problem is not difficult."}]

title: い-adjective past (〜かったです)
pattern: Adj-い → Adj-かったです / くなかったです
meaning: Past tense of い-adjectives (was / was not)
explanation: To form the past of an い-adjective, drop い and add かったです (affirmative) or くなかったです (negative). いい becomes よかったです in the past.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日は寒かったです。","reading":"きのうはさむかったです。","english":"It was cold yesterday."},{"japanese":"映画はあまり面白くなかったです。","reading":"えいがはあまりおもしろくなかったです。","english":"The movie wasn't very interesting."},{"japanese":"テストは難しかったですか。","reading":"テストはむずかしかったですか。","english":"Was the test difficult?"}]

title: な-adjective past (〜でした / じゃなかったです)
pattern: Adj-な → でした / じゃなかったです
meaning: Past tense of な-adjectives (was / was not)
explanation: な-adjectives use でした for the past affirmative and じゃなかったです (or ではありませんでした formally) for the past negative. They conjugate like nouns with the copula.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"その部屋は静かでした。","reading":"そのへやはしずかでした。","english":"That room was quiet."},{"japanese":"子供の頃は元気じゃなかったです。","reading":"こどものころはげんきじゃなかったです。","english":"I wasn't energetic as a child."},{"japanese":"試験は大変でしたか。","reading":"しけんはたいへんでしたか。","english":"Was the exam tough?"}]

title: これ / それ / あれ / どれ (demonstrative pronouns)
pattern: これ / それ / あれ / どれ
meaning: This / that (near listener) / that (far) / which — stand-alone demonstrative pronouns
explanation: これ refers to something near the speaker, それ to something near the listener, あれ to something far from both. どれ asks 'which one' among three or more options.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは何ですか。","reading":"これはなんですか。","english":"What is this?"},{"japanese":"それは私のかばんです。","reading":"それはわたしのかばんです。","english":"That is my bag."},{"japanese":"あれはきれいですね。","reading":"あれはきれいですね。","english":"That (over there) is beautiful, isn't it?"}]

title: この / その / あの / どの + N (demonstrative adjectives)
pattern: この/その/あの/どの + N
meaning: This / that / that (far) / which — demonstratives that directly modify nouns
explanation: Unlike これ/それ/あれ which stand alone, この/その/あの/どの must directly precede a noun. The distance rules are the same: この = near speaker, その = near listener, あの = far from both.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この本は面白いです。","reading":"このほんはおもしろいです。","english":"This book is interesting."},{"japanese":"その映画を見ましたか。","reading":"そのえいがをみましたか。","english":"Did you see that movie?"},{"japanese":"あの山は高いです。","reading":"あのやまはたかいです。","english":"That mountain is tall."}]

title: ここ / そこ / あそこ / どこ (location demonstratives)
pattern: ここ / そこ / あそこ / どこ
meaning: Here / there (near listener) / over there / where
explanation: ここ, そこ, あそこ refer to locations using the same speaker/listener/far distance system as other demonstratives. どこ asks about location ('where?').
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"駅はここです。","reading":"えきはここです。","english":"The station is here."},{"japanese":"トイレはどこですか。","reading":"トイレはどこですか。","english":"Where is the restroom?"},{"japanese":"あそこに座りましょう。","reading":"あそこにすわりましょう。","english":"Let's sit over there."}]

title: 〜から〜まで (from ... to ...)
pattern: N/時間 から N/時間 まで
meaning: From [point] to [point] — marking a range in time or space
explanation: から marks the starting point and まで marks the ending point. They can be used for time (9 o'clock to 5 o'clock) or place (Tokyo to Osaka). They can be used together or separately.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"9時から5時まで働きます。","reading":"くじからごじまではたらきます。","english":"I work from 9 o'clock to 5 o'clock."},{"japanese":"東京から大阪まで新幹線で行きます。","reading":"とうきょうからおおさかまでしんかんせんでいきます。","english":"I go from Tokyo to Osaka by Shinkansen."},{"japanese":"月曜日から金曜日まで学校があります。","reading":"げつようびからきんようびまでがっこうがあります。","english":"There is school from Monday to Friday."}]

title: 〜たい (want to do)
pattern: V-stem + たい
meaning: Expresses the speaker's desire to do something
explanation: たい attaches to the verb stem to express a first-person desire. It conjugates like an い-adjective (たくない, たかった). It should not be used to describe a third person's desire without qualification.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本に行きたいです。","reading":"にほんにいきたいです。","english":"I want to go to Japan."},{"japanese":"何を食べたいですか。","reading":"なにをたべたいですか。","english":"What do you want to eat?"},{"japanese":"早く家に帰りたいです。","reading":"はやくいえにかえりたいです。","english":"I want to go home soon."}]

title: 〜ましょう / 〜ましょうか (let's / shall we?)
pattern: V-stem + ましょう / ましょうか
meaning: Proposes a shared action ('let's do') or offers to do something ('shall I?')
explanation: ましょう is a volitional form expressing 'let's do X together'. ましょうか softens it to a suggestion or offer, meaning 'shall we?' or 'shall I?'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"一緒に行きましょう。","reading":"いっしょにいきましょう。","english":"Let's go together."},{"japanese":"昼ごはんを食べましょうか。","reading":"ひるごはんをたべましょうか。","english":"Shall we eat lunch?"},{"japanese":"始めましょう。","reading":"はじめましょう。","english":"Let's begin."}]

title: 〜ませんか (won't you? / invitation)
pattern: V-stem + ませんか
meaning: Politely invites the listener to do something together
explanation: ませんか is used to make polite invitations or suggestions. It is more tentative and polite than ましょう since it asks for the listener's preference rather than assuming agreement.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"一緒に映画を見ませんか。","reading":"いっしょにえいがをみませんか。","english":"Won't you watch a movie with me?"},{"japanese":"コーヒーを飲みませんか。","reading":"コーヒーをのみませんか。","english":"Would you like to have some coffee?"},{"japanese":"公園を散歩しませんか。","reading":"こうえんをさんぽしませんか。","english":"Shall we take a walk in the park?"}]

title: 〜てください (please do)
pattern: V-て + ください
meaning: Politely requests that someone do something
explanation: て+ください is a polite request form. It is used to ask someone to do an action. For more formal situations, the plain imperative or お〜ください can be used instead.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここに座ってください。","reading":"ここにすわってください。","english":"Please sit here."},{"japanese":"名前を書いてください。","reading":"なまえをかいてください。","english":"Please write your name."},{"japanese":"ゆっくり話してください。","reading":"ゆっくりはなしてください。","english":"Please speak slowly."}]

title: 〜ないでください (please don't)
pattern: V-ない + でください
meaning: Politely requests that someone not do something
explanation: ないで+ください is formed by attaching でください to the plain negative form. It politely asks someone to refrain from an action. More direct than the formal negative imperative.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここで写真を撮らないでください。","reading":"ここでしゃしんをとらないでください。","english":"Please don't take photos here."},{"japanese":"遅刻しないでください。","reading":"ちこくしないでください。","english":"Please don't be late."},{"japanese":"心配しないでください。","reading":"しんぱいしないでください。","english":"Please don't worry."}]

title: 〜が好き / 嫌い / 上手 / 下手 (likes and abilities)
pattern: N が 好き/嫌い/上手/下手 です
meaning: Like / dislike / good at / bad at — uses が to mark the object of feeling or skill
explanation: These expressions use が rather than を to mark their object. 好き and 嫌い express feelings toward something; 上手 and 下手 describe skill level. They are な-adjectives.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"音楽が好きです。","reading":"おんがくがすきです。","english":"I like music."},{"japanese":"料理が上手ですね。","reading":"りょうりがじょうずですね。","english":"You're good at cooking, aren't you?"},{"japanese":"虫が嫌いです。","reading":"むしがきらいです。","english":"I dislike insects."}]

title: 〜ができる (can do / be able to)
pattern: N が できる / V (plain) + ことができる
meaning: Expresses ability or possibility — can do something
explanation: できる can follow a noun (料理ができる = can cook) or こと+が to nominalize a verb phrase (話すことができる = can speak). The noun version is more natural in daily speech.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語が少しできます。","reading":"にほんごがすこしできます。","english":"I can speak a little Japanese."},{"japanese":"料理をすることができません。","reading":"りょうりをすることができません。","english":"I cannot cook."},{"japanese":"早く走ることができますか。","reading":"はやくはしることができますか。","english":"Can you run fast?"}]

title: 〜にあります / います (existence)
pattern: N に N が あります / います
meaning: There is / there are — あります for inanimate objects, います for living beings
explanation: あります is used for non-living things and abstract objects. います is used for people, animals, and other animate beings. Both use に to mark the location of existence.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"机の上に本があります。","reading":"つくえのうえにほんがあります。","english":"There is a book on the desk."},{"japanese":"公園に子供がいます。","reading":"こうえんにこどもがいます。","english":"There are children in the park."},{"japanese":"冷蔵庫にジュースがありますか。","reading":"れいぞうこにジュースがありますか。","english":"Is there any juice in the fridge?"}]

title: 〜ごろ / ぐらい / くらい (approximately)
pattern: 時間/数量 + ごろ / ぐらい / くらい
meaning: Around / about — approximate time or quantity
explanation: ごろ is used for approximate points in time (around 3 o'clock). ぐらい/くらい indicate approximate quantities, durations, or degrees (about 3 hours, about 5 people). They are interchangeable.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"三時ごろ来てください。","reading":"さんじごろきてください。","english":"Please come around 3 o'clock."},{"japanese":"一時間ぐらいかかります。","reading":"いちじかんぐらいかかります。","english":"It takes about an hour."},{"japanese":"五人くらい来ると思います。","reading":"ごにんくらいくるとおもいます。","english":"I think about 5 people will come."}]

title: 〜や〜など (non-exhaustive list)
pattern: N や N など
meaning: Things like X and Y — lists items as examples, implying more exist
explanation: や connects nouns in a partial list, implying other items also exist (unlike と which is exhaustive). など adds 'and so on' or 'for example' at the end to make the non-exhaustive nature explicit.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"りんごやバナナなどを買いました。","reading":"りんごやバナナなどをかいました。","english":"I bought things like apples and bananas."},{"japanese":"日本語や中国語などが話せます。","reading":"にほんごやちゅうごくごなどがはなせます。","english":"I can speak languages like Japanese and Chinese."},{"japanese":"公園や図書館などに行きました。","reading":"こうえんやとしょかんなどにいきました。","english":"I went to places like the park and library."}]

title: 〜前に (before doing)
pattern: V (plain non-past) + 前に / N の 前に
meaning: Before doing / before [noun]
explanation: 前に after a plain non-past verb means 'before doing that action'. After a noun + の, it means 'before [that thing]'. The verb before 前に does not change tense regardless of when the event happens.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"寝る前に歯を磨いてください。","reading":"ねるまえにはをみがいてください。","english":"Please brush your teeth before sleeping."},{"japanese":"食事の前に手を洗います。","reading":"しょくじのまえにてをあらいます。","english":"I wash my hands before meals."},{"japanese":"日本に来る前に日本語を勉強しました。","reading":"にほんにくるまえににほんごをべんきょうしました。","english":"I studied Japanese before coming to Japan."}]

title: 〜後で (after doing)
pattern: V-た + 後で / N の 後で
meaning: After doing — marks a completed action that precedes another
explanation: 後で after a past-tense verb (た-form) means 'after doing'. It implies the first action is fully complete before the second begins, contrasting with てから which has a stronger sequential/causal connection.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"宿題をした後で、遊びます。","reading":"しゅくだいをしたあとで、あそびます。","english":"After doing homework, I will play."},{"japanese":"食事の後で、コーヒーを飲みます。","reading":"しょくじのあとで、コーヒーをのみます。","english":"I drink coffee after meals."},{"japanese":"仕事の後でどこかに行きますか。","reading":"しごとのあとでどこかにいきますか。","english":"Are you going somewhere after work?"}]

title: Question words (いつ / どこ / だれ / なに)
pattern: いつ / どこ / だれ / なに + V/Adj/です
meaning: When / where / who / what — the core question words
explanation: Japanese question words do not move to the front of the sentence the way English 'wh-words' do. They stay in the same position the answer would occupy. か at the end marks it as a question.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"誕生日はいつですか。","reading":"たんじょうびはいつですか。","english":"When is your birthday?"},{"japanese":"お手洗いはどこですか。","reading":"おてあらいはどこですか。","english":"Where is the restroom?"},{"japanese":"それは何ですか。","reading":"それはなんですか。","english":"What is that?"}]

title: て-form + あげる / くれる / もらう (giving & receiving actions)
pattern: V-て + あげる / くれる / もらう
meaning: Do something for someone (giving/receiving the action as a favour)
explanation: あげる = speaker does for someone else; くれる = someone does for the speaker; もらう = speaker receives the action (often = 'have someone do for me'). Direction determines which to use.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"友達に本を貸してあげました。","reading":"ともだちにほんをかしてあげました。","english":"I lent a book to my friend (as a favour)."},{"japanese":"先生が漢字を教えてくれました。","reading":"せんせいがかんじをおしえてくれました。","english":"The teacher taught me kanji (for my benefit)."},{"japanese":"駅まで送ってもらいました。","reading":"えきまでおくってもらいました。","english":"I had someone take me to the station."}]

title: 〜てみる (try doing)
pattern: V-て + みる
meaning: Try doing something to see what happens
explanation: てみる (from みる 'to see') expresses trying an action to experience the result. It implies the outcome is unknown and the speaker is attempting it. みる conjugates normally (みました, みてください, etc.).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この料理を食べてみてください。","reading":"このりょうりをたべてみてください。","english":"Please try eating this dish."},{"japanese":"その映画を見てみました。","reading":"そのえいがをみてみました。","english":"I tried watching that movie."},{"japanese":"新しいカフェに行ってみます。","reading":"あたらしいカフェにいってみます。","english":"I'll try going to the new café."}]

title: 〜ておく (do in advance / preparation)
pattern: V-て + おく
meaning: Do something in advance and leave it ready for later
explanation: ておく expresses doing something as preparation or leaving a completed state. It implies foresight—you do it now so things are ready later. In casual speech it contracts to 〜とく.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"パーティーの前に料理を作っておきます。","reading":"パーティーのまえにりょうりをつくっておきます。","english":"I'll prepare the food in advance for the party."},{"japanese":"旅行の前にホテルを予約しておきました。","reading":"りょこうのまえにホテルをよやくしておきました。","english":"I reserved the hotel in advance before the trip."},{"japanese":"資料を読んでおいてください。","reading":"しりょうをよんでおいてください。","english":"Please read the materials in advance."}]

title: 〜てしまう (completed / regretful action)
pattern: V-て + しまう
meaning: Completely finish doing / end up doing (often with regret or surprise)
explanation: てしまう expresses either a completed action (focus on finality) or an action that happened unintentionally or regrettably. In casual speech it contracts to 〜ちゃう / 〜じゃう.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"宿題を全部やってしまいました。","reading":"しゅくだいをぜんぶやってしまいました。","english":"I finished all the homework (completely)."},{"japanese":"財布を忘れてしまいました。","reading":"さいふをわすれてしまいました。","english":"I went and forgot my wallet."},{"japanese":"ケーキを全部食べてしまった。","reading":"ケーキをぜんぶたべてしまった。","english":"I ended up eating all the cake."}]

title: 〜ていく / てくる (directional continuity)
pattern: V-て + いく / くる
meaning: Do and go away / do and come (expressing action continuing in a direction)
explanation: ていく expresses something moving away or a change progressing into the future. てくる expresses something coming toward the speaker, approaching, or a change coming up to now. Both describe direction of change.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"気温がどんどん上がっていきます。","reading":"きおんがどんどんあがっていきます。","english":"The temperature keeps rising (going forward)."},{"japanese":"日本語が少しずつ上手になってきました。","reading":"にほんごがすこしずつじょうずになってきました。","english":"My Japanese has gradually improved (up to now)."},{"japanese":"弁当を持っていきます。","reading":"べんとうをもっていきます。","english":"I'll take a bento box with me."}]

title: Potential form 〜られる / 〜える (can / be able to)
pattern: V → potential form (-られる / -える)
meaning: Express ability to do something — 'can do'
explanation: Group 1 verbs: replace る with られる (食べる→食べられる). Group 2 verbs: replace the final u sound with the eru form (書く→書ける). Irregular: する→できる, くる→こられる. Colloquially ら is often dropped from Group 1.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語が少し話せます。","reading":"にほんごがすこしはなせます。","english":"I can speak a little Japanese."},{"japanese":"一人で料理が作れますか。","reading":"ひとりでりょうりがつくれますか。","english":"Can you cook by yourself?"},{"japanese":"辛い食べ物が食べられません。","reading":"からいたべものがたべられません。","english":"I cannot eat spicy food."}]

title: Passive form 〜られる (be done to / passive)
pattern: V → passive form (-られる / -される)
meaning: The subject receives the action from someone else
explanation: Group 1: replace る with られる (食べられる = be eaten). Group 2: replace final u with される/われる etc. (書く→書かれる). The agent performing the action is marked by に. Used for direct harm, historical facts, and formal writing.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生に褒められました。","reading":"せんせいにほめられました。","english":"I was praised by the teacher."},{"japanese":"雨に降られて、濡れました。","reading":"あめにふられて、ぬれました。","english":"I got rained on and got wet."},{"japanese":"この小説は多くの人に読まれています。","reading":"このしょうせつはおおくのひとによまれています。","english":"This novel is read by many people."}]

title: Causative form 〜させる (make / let someone do)
pattern: V → causative form (-させる / -せる)
meaning: Make or let someone do something
explanation: Group 1: replace る with させる. Group 2: replace final u vowel with aseru/seru. Irregular: する→させる, くる→こさせる. The person made to do something is marked by に (or を in some cases). Context determines 'make' vs. 'let'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生は学生に作文を書かせました。","reading":"せんせいはがくせいにさくぶんをかかせました。","english":"The teacher made the students write an essay."},{"japanese":"子供に好きなものを食べさせます。","reading":"こどもにすきなものをたべさせます。","english":"I let my child eat what they like."},{"japanese":"部下に残業させてしまいました。","reading":"ぶかにざんぎょうさせてしまいました。","english":"I ended up making my subordinates work overtime."}]

title: 〜と思います (I think that ...)
pattern: S (plain) + と思います
meaning: Express an opinion or conjecture — 'I think that ...'
explanation: と思います follows a plain-form clause to express the speaker's personal opinion or guess. For negative opinions, ないと思います (think not) is more natural than と思いません.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"明日は晴れると思います。","reading":"あしたははれるとおもいます。","english":"I think it will be sunny tomorrow."},{"japanese":"彼は来ないと思います。","reading":"かれはこないとおもいます。","english":"I think he won't come."},{"japanese":"この問題は難しいと思います。","reading":"このもんだいはむずかしいとおもいます。","english":"I think this problem is difficult."}]

title: 〜と言います / と言っていました (say / said)
pattern: 「quote」 と言います / S (plain) + と言っていました
meaning: Say / is called / said that — reporting speech
explanation: と言います reports direct or indirect speech. と言っていました reports what someone was saying (progressive past), often used for hearsay. いう can also mean 'be called' (〜という = called/named).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"田中さんは「明日来ます」と言いました。","reading":"たなかさんは「あしたきます」といいました。","english":"Tanaka-san said 'I'll come tomorrow'."},{"japanese":"これを何と言いますか。","reading":"これをなんといいますか。","english":"What do you call this?"},{"japanese":"先生は試験があると言っていました。","reading":"せんせいはしけんがあるといっていました。","english":"The teacher was saying there would be a test."}]

title: 〜そうです (looks like / I heard that)
pattern: V-stem / Adj-stem + そうです (appearance) / S (plain) + そうです (hearsay)
meaning: Looks like [visual inference] OR I heard that [reported information]
explanation: Two different そうです: (1) Appearance/visual: V-stem or adj-stem + そう (今にも雨が降りそう = looks like it'll rain). (2) Hearsay: plain clause + そうです (田中さんが来るそうです = I heard Tanaka is coming). The forms are distinct.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この料理はおいしそうですね。","reading":"このりょうりはおいしそうですね。","english":"This dish looks delicious."},{"japanese":"明日は雨が降るそうです。","reading":"あしたはあめがふるそうです。","english":"I heard it will rain tomorrow."},{"japanese":"彼女は忙しそうでした。","reading":"かのじょはいそがしそうでした。","english":"She looked busy."}]

title: 〜らしい (apparently / seems like)
pattern: S (plain) + らしい / N + らしい
meaning: Apparently / it seems — inference from evidence or hearsay, or 'typical of'
explanation: らしい has two meanings: (1) Evidential inference based on information ('apparently', 'it seems like') — similar to そうです (hearsay). (2) Typicality: N + らしい means 'typical of N' or '-like'. The context disambiguates.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼女は来ないらしいです。","reading":"かのじょはこないらしいです。","english":"It seems she's not coming."},{"japanese":"あの人は有名な歌手らしいです。","reading":"あのひとはゆうめいなかしゅらしいです。","english":"Apparently that person is a famous singer."},{"japanese":"それは日本らしい風景ですね。","reading":"それはにほんらしいふうけいですね。","english":"That's a very Japanese-like scenery, isn't it?"}]

title: 〜ようです (appears to be / seems)
pattern: S (plain) + ようです / N の + ようです
meaning: It seems / appears — inference based on objective observation
explanation: ようです expresses an inference based on evidence observed by the speaker. It is more formal and objective than みたいです (the casual equivalent). It does not express hearsay like そうです.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼は怒っているようです。","reading":"かれはおこっているようです。","english":"He appears to be angry."},{"japanese":"今日の試験は難しかったようですね。","reading":"きょうのしけんはむずかしかったようですね。","english":"Today's exam seems to have been difficult."},{"japanese":"外は雨のようです。","reading":"そとはあめのようです。","english":"It appears to be raining outside."}]

title: 〜ために (in order to / because of)
pattern: V (plain non-past) + ために [purpose] / N の + ために [cause or benefit]
meaning: For the purpose of / for the sake of / because of
explanation: ために after a plain verb expresses purpose (in order to do). After a noun + の or plain past verb it can express cause (because of) or beneficiary (for the sake of). Subject of main and subordinate clauses must usually differ for purpose use.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語を学ぶために日本に来ました。","reading":"にほんごをまなぶためににほんにきました。","english":"I came to Japan in order to learn Japanese."},{"japanese":"家族のために働いています。","reading":"かぞくのためにはたらいています。","english":"I work for the sake of my family."},{"japanese":"病気のために学校を休みました。","reading":"びょうきのためにがっこうをやすみました。","english":"I was absent from school because of illness."}]

title: 〜ように (so that / in order to)
pattern: V (plain) + ように V (intentional)
meaning: So that / in order that — expresses a goal the speaker works toward
explanation: ように is used when the goal is a state or condition (often using verbs like なる, できる, or potential forms) rather than a direct action. It implies the main action is done so the ように clause becomes true.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"聞こえるように大きな声で話してください。","reading":"きこえるようにおおきなこえではなしてください。","english":"Please speak loudly so that I can hear you."},{"japanese":"忘れないようにメモしました。","reading":"わすれないようにメモしました。","english":"I made a note so that I wouldn't forget."},{"japanese":"日本語が話せるように練習しています。","reading":"にほんごがはなせるようにれんしゅうしています。","english":"I'm practicing so that I can speak Japanese."}]

title: 〜ようになる (come to / reach a state where)
pattern: V (plain) + ようになる / V-potential + ようになる
meaning: Come to do / reach a point where — marks a change of state over time
explanation: ようになる expresses a gradual or eventual change: something that wasn't possible or happening before now is. Often used with potential forms or verbs of perception to describe acquired abilities or habits.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語で夢を見るようになりました。","reading":"にほんごでゆめをみるようになりました。","english":"I've come to dream in Japanese."},{"japanese":"辛い食べ物が食べられるようになりました。","reading":"からいたべものがたべられるようになりました。","english":"I've come to be able to eat spicy food."},{"japanese":"毎朝走るようになりました。","reading":"まいあさはしるようになりました。","english":"I've started running every morning."}]

title: 〜なければならない (must / have to)
pattern: V-なければ + ならない / いけない
meaning: Must do / have to do — obligation
explanation: なければならない expresses strong obligation. The casual form is なきゃ (ならない). ないといけない is an alternative. The choice between ならない and いけない is a matter of style; ならない is slightly more formal.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"毎日薬を飲まなければなりません。","reading":"まいにちくすりをのまなければなりません。","english":"I have to take medicine every day."},{"japanese":"もっと勉強しなければならないと思います。","reading":"もっとべんきょうしなければならないとおもいます。","english":"I think I have to study more."},{"japanese":"明日早く起きなければいけません。","reading":"あしたはやくおきなければいけません。","english":"I have to get up early tomorrow."}]

title: 〜てもいいです (it is okay to / may)
pattern: V-て + もいいです
meaning: Permission — it is okay/allowed to do something
explanation: てもいいです grants or asks for permission to do something. The question form てもいいですか is very common for requesting permission. It can be made stronger with かまいません ('I don't mind').
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここに座ってもいいですか。","reading":"ここにすわってもいいですか。","english":"May I sit here?"},{"japanese":"写真を撮ってもいいですよ。","reading":"しゃしんをとってもいいですよ。","english":"You may take photos."},{"japanese":"帰ってもいいです。","reading":"かえってもいいです。","english":"You may go home."}]

title: 〜てはいけません (must not / prohibited)
pattern: V-て + はいけません / はだめです
meaning: Must not do — prohibition
explanation: てはいけません is a polite prohibition. The casual form is てはだめ or ちゃだめ. It is typically used by authority figures or in rules. It is stronger than ないでください (please don't).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここでタバコを吸ってはいけません。","reading":"ここでタバコをすってはいけません。","english":"You must not smoke here."},{"japanese":"試験中に話してはいけません。","reading":"しけんちゅうにはなしてはいけません。","english":"You must not talk during the exam."},{"japanese":"嘘をついてはだめですよ。","reading":"うそをついてはだめですよ。","english":"You mustn't tell lies."}]

title: 〜たほうがいい (had better / should)
pattern: V-た + ほうがいい (affirmative advice) / V-ない + ほうがいい (negative advice)
meaning: Had better do / you should — giving advice
explanation: たほうがいい (past plain form + ほうがいい) gives advice that something is recommended. For negative advice, use ないほうがいい (shouldn't do). Both are softer than must/have-to expressions.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"早く寝たほうがいいですよ。","reading":"はやくねたほうがいいですよ。","english":"You had better go to sleep early."},{"japanese":"無理をしないほうがいいと思います。","reading":"むりをしないほうがいいとおもいます。","english":"I think you shouldn't push yourself too hard."},{"japanese":"医者に行ったほうがいいですよ。","reading":"いしゃにいったほうがいいですよ。","english":"You should go to the doctor."}]

title: 〜なくてもいいです (don't have to)
pattern: V-なくて + もいいです
meaning: Don't have to / need not — absence of obligation
explanation: なくてもいいです says that something is not required. It is the opposite of なければならない. The casual form is なくていい. Often used to give the listener relief from an expected obligation.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"明日は来なくてもいいです。","reading":"あしたはこなくてもいいです。","english":"You don't have to come tomorrow."},{"japanese":"全部食べなくてもいいですよ。","reading":"ぜんぶたべなくてもいいですよ。","english":"You don't have to eat everything."},{"japanese":"スーツを着なくてもいいですか。","reading":"スーツをきなくてもいいですか。","english":"Is it okay if I don't wear a suit?"}]

title: 〜かもしれません (might / maybe)
pattern: S (plain) + かもしれません
meaning: Might / maybe / possibly — expressing uncertainty
explanation: かもしれません expresses possibility without strong confidence. The plain form is かもしれない (casual: かも). It does not indicate probability as high as でしょう or はずです. Can follow plain-form clauses of any tense.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"明日は雨が降るかもしれません。","reading":"あしたはあめがふるかもしれません。","english":"It might rain tomorrow."},{"japanese":"彼女はもう帰ったかもしれない。","reading":"かのじょはもうかえったかもしれない。","english":"She might have already gone home."},{"japanese":"この薬は効かないかもしれません。","reading":"このくすりはきかないかもしれません。","english":"This medicine might not work."}]

title: 〜でしょう / だろう (probably / right?)
pattern: S (plain) + でしょう / だろう
meaning: Probably / I expect — conjecture, or seeks confirmation (rising intonation)
explanation: でしょう / だろう express the speaker's confident conjecture. With rising intonation (でしょう?), it seeks the listener's agreement. It is more confident than かもしれない. だろう is the plain/casual equivalent.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"明日は晴れるでしょう。","reading":"あしたははれるでしょう。","english":"It will probably be sunny tomorrow."},{"japanese":"彼は知っているでしょう？","reading":"かれはしっているでしょう？","english":"He probably knows, right?"},{"japanese":"試験は難しかっただろう。","reading":"しけんはむずかしかっただろう。","english":"The exam was probably difficult."}]

title: 〜ば conditional (if)
pattern: V-ば / Adj-ければ / N + であれば
meaning: If [condition], [result] — general or hypothetical conditional
explanation: ば expresses a general or hypothetical condition: 'if A, then B'. Both the condition and result can be hypothetical. It often implies the result naturally follows from the condition. Not used for habitual conditions (use と) or one-time future events as naturally as たら.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"安ければ買います。","reading":"やすければかいます。","english":"If it's cheap, I'll buy it."},{"japanese":"時間があれば手伝います。","reading":"じかんがあればてつだいます。","english":"If I have time, I'll help."},{"japanese":"この薬を飲めば治るでしょう。","reading":"このくすりをのめばなおるでしょう。","english":"If you take this medicine, you'll probably get better."}]

title: 〜たら conditional (when/if — after completion)
pattern: V-たら / Adj-かったら / N + だったら
meaning: When/if [action or state is completed], then — sequential or hypothetical
explanation: たら is the most versatile conditional. It can express: (1) sequence: 'once A happens, B'; (2) hypothetical: 'if A were the case, B'; (3) discovery: 'when I did A, I found B'. It focuses on the completion of the condition.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"家に着いたら電話してください。","reading":"いえについたらでんわしてください。","english":"Please call me when you get home."},{"japanese":"お金があったら旅行したいです。","reading":"おかねがあったらりょこうしたいです。","english":"If I had money, I'd want to travel."},{"japanese":"窓を開けたら、鳥が入ってきました。","reading":"まどをあけたら、とりがはいってきました。","english":"When I opened the window, a bird came in."}]

title: 〜なら conditional (if that's the case)
pattern: S (plain) + なら / N + なら
meaning: If that is the case / if we're talking about — contextual conditional
explanation: なら is used when the condition is something already known from context, the conversation, or what the listener said. It means 'if what you say is true / given that context'. It is not used for things the speaker discovers independently.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"東京に行くなら、スカイツリーを見てください。","reading":"とうきょうにいくなら、スカイツリーをみてください。","english":"If you're going to Tokyo, please see the Sky Tree."},{"japanese":"日本語が難しいなら、一緒に勉強しましょう。","reading":"にほんごがむずかしいなら、いっしょにべんきょうしましょう。","english":"If Japanese is difficult, let's study together."},{"japanese":"彼女が行かないなら、私も行きません。","reading":"かのじょがいかないなら、わたしもいきません。","english":"If she's not going, I won't go either."}]

title: 〜ても (even if / even though)
pattern: V-て + も / Adj-くても / N + でも
meaning: Even if / even though — the main clause holds regardless of the condition
explanation: ても indicates a concessive condition: regardless of A, B still holds. It is used for hypothetical situations (even if X happens, Y is still the case) and actual concessions (even though X is happening, Y).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"雨が降っても、試合は続けます。","reading":"あめがふっても、しあいはつづけます。","english":"Even if it rains, we'll continue the game."},{"japanese":"高くても、買いたいです。","reading":"たかくても、かいたいです。","english":"Even if it's expensive, I want to buy it."},{"japanese":"疲れていても、仕事をしなければなりません。","reading":"つかれていても、しごとをしなければなりません。","english":"Even though I'm tired, I have to work."}]

title: 〜のに (even though — unexpected / regret)
pattern: S (plain) + のに
meaning: Even though / despite — expresses surprise, dissatisfaction, or regret
explanation: のに is a concessive that carries emotional weight: the speaker finds the result unexpected, disappointing, or contrary to what they expected. It differs from ても in that ても is neutral while のに implies the speaker's feelings.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"たくさん勉強したのに、試験に落ちました。","reading":"たくさんべんきょうしたのに、しけんにおちました。","english":"Even though I studied a lot, I failed the exam."},{"japanese":"せっかく来たのに、彼女はいませんでした。","reading":"せっかくきたのに、かのじょはいませんでした。","english":"I went to all the trouble of coming, but she wasn't there."},{"japanese":"あんなに練習したのに、うまくできませんでした。","reading":"あんなにれんしゅうしたのに、うまくできませんでした。","english":"Despite all that practice, I couldn't do it well."}]

title: 〜から (because — reason/cause)
pattern: S (plain/polite) + から
meaning: Because / since — states a reason for the main clause
explanation: から gives a reason or cause. It can follow plain or polite forms (both are acceptable). The reason clause comes first. It is more subjective and direct than ので, and can follow complete sentences.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"眠いから、早く寝ます。","reading":"ねむいから、はやくねます。","english":"I'll go to bed early because I'm sleepy."},{"japanese":"お腹が空いているから、何か食べましょう。","reading":"おなかがすいているから、なにかたべましょう。","english":"Since I'm hungry, let's eat something."},{"japanese":"日本語が好きだから、毎日勉強しています。","reading":"にほんごがすきだから、まいにちべんきょうしています。","english":"Because I like Japanese, I study every day."}]

title: 〜ので (because — softer reason)
pattern: S (plain) + ので / Adj-な + なので / N + なので
meaning: Because / since — gives a reason with softer, more polite tone
explanation: ので is similar to から but carries a softer, more objective tone. It sounds less assertive and is preferred in formal/polite contexts. な-adjectives and nouns use なので before it.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"体調が悪いので、早退してもいいですか。","reading":"たいちょうがわるいので、そうたいしてもいいですか。","english":"May I leave early since I'm not feeling well?"},{"japanese":"明日試験があるので、今夜は勉強します。","reading":"あしたしけんがあるので、こんやはべんきょうします。","english":"I'll study tonight because there's an exam tomorrow."},{"japanese":"雨なので、傘を持って行ってください。","reading":"あめなので、かさをもっていってください。","english":"Please bring an umbrella since it's raining."}]

title: 〜時 (when / at the time of)
pattern: V (plain) / Adj / N の + 時
meaning: When / at the time of — marks a point in time or circumstance
explanation: 時 follows a clause to mean 'when A [happens/happened]'. If the 時 clause uses the same tense as the main clause, they are simultaneous. The relationship between tenses of 時 clause and main clause conveys important timing nuance.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"子供の時、よく海で泳ぎました。","reading":"こどものとき、よくうみでおよぎました。","english":"When I was a child, I often swam in the sea."},{"japanese":"困った時は、相談してください。","reading":"こまったときは、そうだんしてください。","english":"When you're in trouble, please consult me."},{"japanese":"日本に来る時、何を持ってきましたか。","reading":"にほんにくるとき、なにをもってきましたか。","english":"What did you bring when you came to Japan?"}]

title: 〜てから (after doing — emphasising sequence)
pattern: V-て + から
meaning: After doing — emphasises a strict temporal sequence
explanation: てから is stronger than 後で: it emphasises that the first action must be fully completed before the second begins. 後で can be separated in time; てから implies immediate succession or strict ordering.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"手を洗ってから、食べます。","reading":"てをあらってから、たべます。","english":"After washing my hands, I'll eat."},{"japanese":"よく考えてから、決めてください。","reading":"よくかんがえてから、きめてください。","english":"Please decide after thinking it through carefully."},{"japanese":"大学を卒業してから、就職しました。","reading":"だいがくをそつぎょうしてから、しゅうしょくしました。","english":"After graduating from university, I found a job."}]

title: 〜という N (a N called / which says)
pattern: 「word/S」 + という + N
meaning: A [N] called / known as / which says — attaches a name or description to a noun
explanation: という connects a quoted name, description, or reported content to a following noun. 「東京」という都市 = 'a city called Tokyo'. It can also quote the content of a sentence: 「明日来る」という連絡 = 'a message saying he'll come tomorrow'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"「さくら」という名前の犬を飼っています。","reading":「「さくら」というなまえのいぬをかっています。","english":"I have a dog named Sakura."},{"japanese":"「JLPT」という試験を知っていますか。","reading":"「JLPT」というしけんをしっていますか。","english":"Do you know the exam called JLPT?"},{"japanese":"明日休むという連絡が来ました。","reading":"あしたやすむというれんらくがきました。","english":"A message came saying he'll be absent tomorrow."}]

title: 〜ことができる (can do — formal ability)
pattern: V (plain non-past) + ことができる
meaning: Can / be able to — more formal than the potential verb form
explanation: ことができる is the formal/written equivalent of the potential verb form. It nominalizes the verb with こと and then applies できる. It is more verbose but used in formal writing, contracts, and set expressions.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語で手紙を書くことができます。","reading":"にほんごでてがみをかくことができます。","english":"I am able to write a letter in Japanese."},{"japanese":"この建物に入ることができません。","reading":"このたてもののにはいることができません。","english":"You cannot enter this building."},{"japanese":"一人で料理することができますか。","reading":"ひとりでりょうりすることができますか。","english":"Can you cook by yourself?"}]

title: 〜ことがある (there are times when / have done before)
pattern: V (plain non-past) + ことがある [sometimes] / V-た + ことがある [experience]
meaning: Sometimes do / have ever done — habitual or experiential
explanation: Non-past + ことがある = 'there are times when I do X / I sometimes do X'. Past + ことがある = 'I have the experience of doing X (at least once)'. The tense of the verb before こと is key.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"電車の中で眠ってしまうことがあります。","reading":"でんしゃのなかでねむってしまうことがあります。","english":"There are times when I fall asleep on the train."},{"japanese":"富士山に登ったことがありますか。","reading":"ふじさんにのぼったことがありますか。","english":"Have you ever climbed Mt. Fuji?"},{"japanese":"日本語で夢を見ることがあります。","reading":"にほんごでゆめをみることがあります。","english":"I sometimes dream in Japanese."}]

title: 〜ことにする (decide to)
pattern: V (plain) + ことにする
meaning: Decide to do — marks a personal decision
explanation: ことにする indicates a decision or resolution made by the speaker. ことにしている (habitual form) means 'I make it a rule to / I always do X'. It focuses on the decision-making moment, not an already existing rule.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"毎朝ジョギングすることにしました。","reading":"まいあさジョギングすることにしました。","english":"I have decided to jog every morning."},{"japanese":"お酒を飲まないことにしました。","reading":"おさけをのまないことにしました。","english":"I've decided not to drink alcohol."},{"japanese":"健康のために野菜を食べることにしています。","reading":"けんこうのためにやさいをたべることにしています。","english":"I make it a rule to eat vegetables for my health."}]

title: 〜ことになる (it has been decided that)
pattern: V (plain) + ことになる / ことになっている
meaning: It has been decided / it turns out that — external decision or established rule
explanation: ことになる expresses an outcome that was decided by external circumstances or others (not the speaker's personal decision — that is ことにする). ことになっている means 'it has been arranged that / it is a rule that'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"来月から東京で働くことになりました。","reading":"らいげつからとうきょうではたらくことになりました。","english":"It has been decided that I'll work in Tokyo from next month."},{"japanese":"この道は夜間通行禁止ということになっています。","reading":"このみちはやかんつうこうきんしということになっています。","english":"It is an established rule that this road is off-limits at night."},{"japanese":"会議は毎週月曜日に行うことになっています。","reading":"かいぎはまいしゅうげつようびにおこなうことになっています。","english":"Meetings are scheduled to be held every Monday."}]

title: 〜だけ (only / just)
pattern: N / V (plain) + だけ
meaning: Only / just — limits the scope to the item or action mentioned
explanation: だけ marks a limit: 'only X, nothing else'. It can modify nouns, plain-form verbs, and adjectives. It is more neutral in tone than しか〜ない (which always appears with a negative and implies insufficiency).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"一つだけ質問があります。","reading":"ひとつだけしつもんがあります。","english":"I only have one question."},{"japanese":"できるだけ早く来てください。","reading":"できるだけはやくきてください。","english":"Please come as quickly as possible."},{"japanese":"見るだけで、買いません。","reading":"みるだけで、かいません。","english":"I'll just look; I won't buy anything."}]

title: 〜しか〜ない (nothing but / only — with negative)
pattern: N / V + しか + negative verb
meaning: Only (with negative) — emphasises insufficiency or restriction
explanation: しか must always be paired with a negative verb. It implies that the amount or option is insufficient or exclusive: 'there is nothing but X' or 'I can only do X (and that's not enough)'. More emphatic than だけ.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"財布に100円しかありません。","reading":"さいふに100えんしかありません。","english":"I only have 100 yen in my wallet."},{"japanese":"ここにはお茶しかありませんよ。","reading":"ここにはおちゃしかありませんよ。","english":"There's only tea here."},{"japanese":"日本語は少ししか話せません。","reading":"にほんごはすこししかはなせません。","english":"I can only speak a little Japanese."}]

title: 〜ばかり (nothing but / just did / full of)
pattern: V-た + ばかり [just did] / N + ばかり [nothing but] / V + ばかりいる [do nothing but]
meaning: Just did / nothing but / full of — various senses of exclusivity or recency
explanation: ばかり has multiple uses: (1) V-た + ばかり = just finished (very recently). (2) N + ばかり = nothing but, only (disparaging). (3) V + ばかりいる = does nothing but. Context determines which meaning applies.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今日本に着いたばかりです。","reading":"いまにほんについたばかりです。","english":"I just arrived in Japan."},{"japanese":"彼はゲームばかりしています。","reading":"かれはゲームばかりしています。","english":"He does nothing but play games."},{"japanese":"この店は外国人ばかりです。","reading":"このみせはがいこくじんばかりです。","english":"This shop is full of foreigners."}]

title: 〜間に (while / during — a change occurs)
pattern: V-ている + 間に / N の 間に
meaning: While / during — something else happens in that interval
explanation: 間に (あいだに) expresses that a separate event occurs during the time span of another. The main clause action often happens without the subject's awareness or as a separate event. Different from 〜ながら which requires the same subject.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生が話している間に、メモを取りました。","reading":"せんせいがはなしているあいだに、メモをとりました。","english":"While the teacher was talking, I took notes."},{"japanese":"休みの間に、本を10冊読みました。","reading":"やすみのあいだに、ほんを10さつよみました。","english":"During the break, I read 10 books."},{"japanese":"出かけている間に、荷物が届きました。","reading":"でかけているあいだに、にもつがとどきました。","english":"While I was out, a package arrived."}]

title: 〜ながら (while doing two things simultaneously)
pattern: V-stem + ながら + V (main action)
meaning: While doing — two actions performed simultaneously by the same subject
explanation: ながら connects two actions done by the same person at the same time. The main action is the second verb; ながら modifies it. It cannot be used when the subject is different or when actions are sequential.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"音楽を聴きながら勉強します。","reading":"おんがくをききながらべんきょうします。","english":"I study while listening to music."},{"japanese":"歩きながら話しましょう。","reading":"あるきながらはなしましょう。","english":"Let's talk while walking."},{"japanese":"コーヒーを飲みながら、本を読みました。","reading":"コーヒーをのみながら、ほんをよみました。","english":"I read a book while drinking coffee."}]

title: 〜まで (until / as far as)
pattern: N / V (plain) + まで
meaning: Until / up to / as far as — endpoint in time, place, or degree
explanation: まで marks an endpoint: until a time, up to a place, or to the extent that. Combined with から it creates from...to ranges. Also used in expressions like こんなに...まで (to such an extent as this).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"駅まで歩きます。","reading":"えきまであるきます。","english":"I'll walk as far as the station."},{"japanese":"試験が終わるまで、ここにいてください。","reading":"しけんがおわるまで、ここにいてください。","english":"Please stay here until the exam ends."},{"japanese":"夜中まで働きました。","reading":"よなかまではたらきました。","english":"I worked until the middle of the night."}]

title: 〜でも (even / or something — softening)
pattern: N + でも / V-て + も + even sense
meaning: Even [N] / [N] or something — softens suggestions or marks an extreme case
explanation: でも after a noun means 'even' (extreme case) or 'or something' (softening a suggestion). コーヒーでも飲みましょう = 'Let's have some coffee or something'. It does not mean 'but' in this construction.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"コーヒーでも飲みましょうか。","reading":"コーヒーでものみましょうか。","english":"Shall we have some coffee or something?"},{"japanese":"子供でもできる問題です。","reading":"こどもでもできるもんだいです。","english":"It's a problem even a child can solve."},{"japanese":"散歩でもしませんか。","reading":"さんぽでもしませんか。","english":"How about taking a walk or something?"}]

title: 〜し〜し (listing reasons / and besides)
pattern: S (plain) + し、S (plain) + し
meaning: And besides / for one thing ... for another — lists multiple reasons or qualities
explanation: し connects multiple reasons or qualities, implying there are more than just those listed. It adds a cumulative or enumerative tone. Often used to justify a conclusion by stacking evidence.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"安いし、おいしいし、また行きたいです。","reading":"やすいし、おいしいし、またいきたいです。","english":"It's cheap, it's delicious, and I want to go again."},{"japanese":"彼は頭がいいし、優しいし、人気があります。","reading":"かれはあたまがいいし、やさしいし、にんきがあります。","english":"He's smart, kind, and popular."},{"japanese":"疲れているし、眠いし、もう帰ります。","reading":"つかれているし、ねむいし、もうかえります。","english":"I'm tired and sleepy, so I'm going home."}]

title: 〜によると (according to)
pattern: N + によると / によれば
meaning: According to [source] — introduces reported information
explanation: によると introduces information attributed to a source. It is followed by そうです or similar hearsay expressions. によれば is slightly more formal. The speaker does not personally verify the information.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"天気予報によると、明日は雨だそうです。","reading":"てんきよほうによると、あしたはあめだそうです。","english":"According to the weather forecast, it will rain tomorrow."},{"japanese":"彼によると、パーティーは中止だそうです。","reading":"かれによると、パーティーはちゅうしだそうです。","english":"According to him, the party has been cancelled."},{"japanese":"ニュースによれば、事故があったそうです。","reading":"ニュースによれば、じこがあったそうです。","english":"According to the news, there was an accident."}]

title: 〜について (about / concerning)
pattern: N + について / についての + N
meaning: About / concerning / regarding [topic]
explanation: について marks the topic of discussion, thinking, writing, or research. についての modifies a following noun (a report about/on...). It is neutral in formality and widely used in both spoken and written Japanese.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本の文化について話しました。","reading":"にほんのぶんかについてはなしました。","english":"We talked about Japanese culture."},{"japanese":"この問題についてどう思いますか。","reading":"このもんだいについてどうおもいますか。","english":"What do you think about this issue?"},{"japanese":"日本語についての本を読んでいます。","reading":"にほんごについてのほんをよんでいます。","english":"I'm reading a book about the Japanese language."}]

title: 〜ほうがいい vs 〜ほうが (comparison)
pattern: N/V + のほうが + Adj / A より B のほうが
meaning: B is more [adj] than A — comparative structure
explanation: 〜より〜のほうが is the standard comparative: 'B is more [adj] than A'. より marks the reference point (the lesser of the two), and ほうが marks what's being compared favourably.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"バスより電車のほうが速いです。","reading":"バスよりでんしゃのほうがはやいです。","english":"The train is faster than the bus."},{"japanese":"一人より友達と行ったほうが楽しいです。","reading":"ひとりよりともだちといったほうがたのしいです。","english":"It's more fun to go with friends than alone."},{"japanese":"夏より冬のほうが好きです。","reading":"なつよりふゆのほうがすきです。","english":"I prefer winter to summer."}]

title: 〜させていただく (humbly do — formal request)
pattern: V-causative + いただく
meaning: Humbly allow me to do / I will humbly do — formal/polite action request
explanation: させていただく combines the causative (させる = be made/allowed to do) with いただく (humble form of もらう). It humbly requests permission for an action or announces one's own action politely. Very common in formal and business contexts.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"では、説明させていただきます。","reading":"では、せつめいさせていただきます。","english":"Well then, allow me to explain."},{"japanese":"本日は早退させていただきます。","reading":"ほんじつはそうたいさせていただきます。","english":"I will humbly take my leave early today."},{"japanese":"少し考えさせていただけますか。","reading":"すこしかんがえさせていただけますか。","english":"Would you allow me a moment to think about it?"}]

title: 〜ていただけますか (would you please — polite request)
pattern: V-て + いただけますか / もらえますか
meaning: Could you please do — polite/humble request for someone to do something
explanation: ていただけますか is the polite request form combining te-form + いただく (humble 'receive') + potential + question. More polite than てください. Used when asking someone of higher status or a stranger to do something.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"もう一度説明していただけますか。","reading":"もういちどせつめいしていただけますか。","english":"Could you please explain that once more?"},{"japanese":"こちらにサインしていただけますか。","reading":"こちらにサインしていただけますか。","english":"Could you please sign here?"},{"japanese":"少し待っていただけますか。","reading":"すこしまっていただけますか。","english":"Could you please wait a moment?"}]

title: 〜てしまう / 〜ちゃう (regret or completion)
pattern: V-て + しまう / ちゃう (casual)
meaning: Ended up doing / completely did — often with regret or unintended nuance
explanation: てしまう expresses completion with finality or regret. In casual speech it contracts to ちゃう (for て-form) or じゃう (for で-form). Context determines whether the nuance is pure completion or unfortunate outcome.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"また遅刻してしまいました。","reading":"またちこくしてしまいました。","english":"I went and was late again."},{"japanese":"ケーキを全部食べちゃった。","reading":"ケーキをぜんぶたべちゃった。","english":"I ended up eating all the cake."},{"japanese":"大切な書類をなくしてしまいました。","reading":"たいせつなしょるいをなくしてしまいました。","english":"I went and lost the important documents."}]

title: 〜たまま (remaining in a state)
pattern: V-た + まま / N の + まま
meaning: Leaving something in the state it was — without changing it back
explanation: たまま expresses that a state resulting from an action remains unchanged. Often implies negligence or a state persisting longer than expected. The clause before まま uses the た-form.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"電気をつけたまま寝てしまいました。","reading":"でんきをつけたままねてしまいました。","english":"I fell asleep with the light still on."},{"japanese":"靴を履いたまま部屋に入らないでください。","reading":"くつをはいたままへやにはいらないでください。","english":"Please don't enter the room with your shoes on."},{"japanese":"窓が開いたままになっています。","reading":"まどがあいたままになっています。","english":"The window has been left open."}]

title: 〜ずに / 〜ないで (without doing)
pattern: V-ず + に / V-ない + で
meaning: Without doing — performs main action while omitting the subsidiary action
explanation: ずに is the classical/formal equivalent of ないで, both meaning 'without doing X'. ずに sounds slightly more formal or literary. Both indicate the main action is performed while X is not done. する becomes せずに.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"朝ごはんを食べずに学校に行きました。","reading":"あさごはんをたべずにがっこうにいきました。","english":"I went to school without eating breakfast."},{"japanese":"何も言わずに帰ってしまいました。","reading":"なにもいわずにかえってしまいました。","english":"He left without saying anything."},{"japanese":"傘を持たないで出かけました。","reading":"かさをもたないででかけました。","english":"I went out without bringing an umbrella."}]

title: 〜てばかりいる (doing nothing but)
pattern: V-て + ばかりいる
meaning: Do nothing but — criticism that someone only ever does one thing
explanation: てばかりいる combines てform + ばかり (nothing but) + いる (ongoing state). It carries a critical or lamenting tone, implying the person should be doing other things instead.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼はゲームばかりしていて、勉強しない。","reading":"かれはゲームばかりしていて、べんきょうしない。","english":"He does nothing but play games and won't study."},{"japanese":"泣いてばかりいないで、前を向こう。","reading":"ないてばかりいないで、まえをむこう。","english":"Stop just crying and look forward."},{"japanese":"食べてばかりいると太りますよ。","reading":"たべてばかりいるとふとりますよ。","english":"If you do nothing but eat, you'll gain weight."}]

title: 〜てしかたがない / てたまらない (unbearably / can't help but feel)
pattern: V-て / Adj-くて + しかたがない / たまらない
meaning: Unbearably / so X that one can't stand it — strong emotional or physical sensation
explanation: Both expressions convey an overwhelming feeling or sensation the speaker cannot control. たまらない is stronger and can also mean 'can't resist / love it'. しかたがない is more neutral (can't be helped).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この映画は面白くてたまりません。","reading":"このえいがはおもしろくてたまりません。","english":"This movie is so interesting I can't stand it."},{"japanese":"試験が心配でしかたがないです。","reading":"しけんがしんぱいでしかたがないです。","english":"I can't help worrying about the exam."},{"japanese":"辛いものが食べたくてたまらない。","reading":"からいものがたべたくてたまらない。","english":"I can't resist wanting to eat spicy food."}]

title: 〜させてもらう / させてくれる (allow me to)
pattern: V-causative + もらう / くれる
meaning: Allow me to do / let me do — asking or being given permission
explanation: させてもらう (humble: I receive permission to do) and させてくれる (someone kindly lets me do) are used to request or express gratitude for being allowed to do something. Common in polite or business contexts.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"少し休ませてもらえますか。","reading":"すこしやすませてもらえますか。","english":"Would you let me rest a little?"},{"japanese":"先生が発表させてくれました。","reading":"せんせいがはっぴょうさせてくれました。","english":"The teacher let me give the presentation."},{"japanese":"このプロジェクトに参加させてください。","reading":"このプロジェクトにさんかさせてください。","english":"Please let me participate in this project."}]

title: 〜させられる (involuntary causative-passive)
pattern: V-causative-passive (-させられる / -される)
meaning: Be made to do — the subject is forced by someone else to do something
explanation: The causative-passive combines causative (させる) and passive (られる). It means the subject is compelled by someone else to perform the action, often against their will. Group 2 verbs contract: 食べさせられる, 飲まされる.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"子供の頃、野菜を食べさせられました。","reading":"こどものころ、やさいをたべさせられました。","english":"As a child, I was made to eat vegetables."},{"japanese":"毎日残業させられて、疲れました。","reading":"まいにちざんぎょうさせられて、つかれました。","english":"I've been made to work overtime every day and I'm exhausted."},{"japanese":"先生に廊下に立たされました。","reading":"せんせいにろうかにたたされました。","english":"I was made to stand in the hallway by the teacher."}]

title: 〜らしい (typical of / -like)
pattern: N + らしい (typicality sense)
meaning: Typical of / befitting — something is characteristic or expected of its category
explanation: This is the typicality sense of らしい (distinct from its evidential sense). N + らしい means 'typical of N' or 'like what N should be'. 男らしい = manly/masculine, 日本らしい = typically Japanese.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"それは彼らしい答えですね。","reading":"それはかれらしいこたえですね。","english":"That's a very typical answer for him."},{"japanese":"春らしい暖かい日が続いています。","reading":"はるらしいあたたかいひがつづいています。","english":"Warm days typical of spring continue."},{"japanese":"女性らしい優しい気遣いに感動しました。","reading":"じょせいらしいやさしいきづかいにかんどうしました。","english":"I was moved by her characteristically feminine gentle consideration."}]

title: 〜みたいだ (seems like / like — casual)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + みたいだ / N + みたいだ
meaning: Seems like / looks like / like — casual inference or comparison
explanation: みたいだ is the casual equivalent of ようだ for expressing inference or comparison. みたいな can modify nouns (子供みたいな行動 = child-like behavior). It is not used in formal writing.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼は怒っているみたいですね。","reading":"かれはおこっているみたいですね。","english":"He seems angry."},{"japanese":"まるで夢みたいな話ですね。","reading":"まるでゆめみたいなはなしですね。","english":"It sounds like a dream."},{"japanese":"外は雨が降っているみたいです。","reading":"そとはあめがふっているみたいです。","english":"It looks like it's raining outside."}]

title: 〜はずだ (should be / expected to)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + はずだ / N の + はずだ
meaning: Should be / expected to be — logical expectation based on knowledge
explanation: はずだ expresses the speaker's strong expectation based on knowledge or reasoning. It is more confident than かもしれない and implies there is a logical basis. はずだったのに expresses unfulfilled expectation with disappointment.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼はもう着いているはずです。","reading":"かれはもうついているはずです。","english":"He should have arrived by now."},{"japanese":"この薬を飲めば治るはずです。","reading":"このくすりをのめばなおるはずです。","english":"If you take this medicine, you should recover."},{"japanese":"鍵はここにあるはずなのに、見つかりません。","reading":"かぎはここにあるはずなのに、みつかりません。","english":"The key should be here, but I can't find it."}]

title: 〜わけだ (that's why / it means that)
pattern: S (plain) + わけだ
meaning: That's why / it makes sense that — logical conclusion from facts
explanation: わけだ presents a natural conclusion following from given information. It expresses that something makes sense or is understandable given the circumstances. Different from はずだ (expectation) — わけだ is a logical derivation.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"10年間日本に住んでいたわけだから、日本語が上手なわけだ。","reading":"10ねんかんにほんにすんでいたわけだから、にほんごがじょうずなわけだ。","english":"He lived in Japan for 10 years, so of course his Japanese is good."},{"japanese":"昨日何も食べなかったわけだから、お腹が空くわけです。","reading":"きのうなにもたべなかったわけだから、おなかがすくわけです。","english":"I didn't eat anything yesterday, so naturally I'm hungry."},{"japanese":"なるほど、そういうわけだったんですね。","reading":"なるほど、そういうわけだったんですね。","english":"I see, so that's what it was all about."}]

title: 〜わけではない (it doesn't mean that)
pattern: S (plain) + わけではない / わけじゃない
meaning: It's not the case that / it doesn't mean — partial negation or clarification
explanation: わけではない partially negates: it doesn't mean X (but it could partially be true). Used to soften or clarify. Different from ではない (flat denial) — わけではない says 'don't take it that far'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"嫌いなわけではないけど、得意じゃないです。","reading":"きらいなわけではないけど、とくいじゃないです。","english":"It's not that I dislike it, I'm just not good at it."},{"japanese":"忙しくないわけではないんですが、行けます。","reading":"いそがしくないわけではないんですが、いけます。","english":"It's not that I'm not busy, but I can go."},{"japanese":"諦めたわけじゃない。","reading":"あきらめたわけじゃない。","english":"It doesn't mean I've given up."}]

title: 〜に違いない (must be / no doubt)
pattern: S (plain) + に違いない
meaning: Must be / certainly — strong conviction based on reasoning
explanation: に違いない expresses near-certainty based on the speaker's reasoning or observation. It is stronger than はずだ and implies the speaker is very confident. Cannot be used when the speaker has direct evidence (use だ then).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"こんなに疲れているから、昨日はよく眠れなかったに違いない。","reading":"こんなにつかれているから、きのうはよくねむれなかったにちがいない。","english":"He must not have slept well last night since he's so tired."},{"japanese":"あの二人は知り合いに違いありません。","reading":"あのふたりはしりあいにちがいありません。","english":"Those two must know each other."},{"japanese":"彼女は合格したに違いない。","reading":"かのじょはごうかくしたにちがいない。","english":"She must have passed."}]

title: 〜によって (depending on / by means of)
pattern: N + によって / による + N
meaning: By / depending on / due to — marks agent, means, or variation by factor
explanation: によって has multiple uses: (1) passive agent (written/formal alternative to に); (2) means or method; (3) variation depending on a factor. による is the attributive form (preceding a noun).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この小説はその作家によって書かれました。","reading":"このしょうせつはそのさっかによってかかれました。","english":"This novel was written by that author."},{"japanese":"結果は努力によって変わります。","reading":"けっかはどりょくによってかわります。","english":"Results change depending on effort."},{"japanese":"地震による被害が出ています。","reading":"じしんによるひがいがでています。","english":"Damage caused by the earthquake is being reported."}]

title: 〜に対して (toward / in contrast to)
pattern: N + に対して / に対する + N
meaning: Toward / against / in contrast to — marks target of action or feeling, or contrast
explanation: に対して marks the target or recipient of an action, attitude, or feeling. It can also mean 'in contrast to / whereas' when comparing two things. に対する is the attributive form.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生に対して失礼なことを言ってしまいました。","reading":"せんせいにたいしてしつれいなことをいってしまいました。","english":"I said something rude toward the teacher."},{"japanese":"弟は内向的なのに対して、姉は社交的です。","reading":"おとうとはないこうてきなのにたいして、あねはしゃこうてきです。","english":"My brother is introverted, whereas my sister is sociable."},{"japanese":"環境問題に対する意識が高まっています。","reading":"かんきょうもんだいにたいするいしきがたかまっています。","english":"Awareness toward environmental issues is growing."}]

title: 〜として (as / in the role of)
pattern: N + として
meaning: As / in the capacity of / treating as — marks a role, function, or classification
explanation: として marks the role, capacity, or category in which something acts or is treated. 友達として = as a friend; 問題として取り上げる = take up as a problem. It can also mean 'as' in comparisons (standard/basis).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼は代表として会議に出席しました。","reading":"かれはだいひょうとしてかいぎにしゅっせきしました。","english":"He attended the meeting as a representative."},{"japanese":"それは問題として取り上げるべきです。","reading":"それはもんだいとしてとりあげるべきです。","english":"That should be taken up as an issue."},{"japanese":"友達として言うけど、少し休んだほうがいいよ。","reading":"ともだちとしていうけど、すこしやすんだほうがいいよ。","english":"As a friend I'm telling you, you should rest a bit."}]

title: 〜にとって (for / from the standpoint of)
pattern: N + にとって
meaning: For / from the perspective of — marks who experiences or judges something
explanation: にとって marks whose perspective or interest is relevant: 'for [person], this is...'. It differs from のために (purpose/benefit) — にとって is about perspective and assessment, not action.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この仕事は私にとって大切な経験です。","reading":"このしごとはわたしにとってたいせつなけいけんです。","english":"This job is an important experience for me."},{"japanese":"子供にとって遊びは学習の一部です。","reading":"こどもにとってあそびはがくしゅうのいちぶです。","english":"For children, play is part of learning."},{"japanese":"外国語の習得は誰にとっても難しいです。","reading":"がいこくごのしゅうとくはだれにとってもむずかしいです。","english":"Acquiring a foreign language is difficult for anyone."}]

title: 〜だけでなく〜も (not only ... but also)
pattern: N / V (plain) + だけでなく + N / S + も
meaning: Not only ... but also — adds an additional unexpected element
explanation: だけでなく〜も indicates that something applies not only to the first element but also to the second. The second element (marked by も) is usually more surprising or significant. ばかりでなく is a near-synonym.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"彼女は歌が上手なだけでなく、ダンスも得意です。","reading":"かのじょはうたがじょうずなだけでなく、ダンスもとくいです。","english":"Not only is she good at singing, but she's also good at dancing."},{"japanese":"この店は安いだけでなく、品質も高いです。","reading":"このみせはやすいだけでなく、ひんしつもたかいです。","english":"This shop is not only cheap but also high quality."},{"japanese":"日本語だけでなく、中国語も話せます。","reading":"にほんごだけでなく、ちゅうごくごもはなせます。","english":"I can speak not only Japanese but also Chinese."}]

title: 〜ほど (to the degree that / the more ... the more)
pattern: N + ほど / V (plain) + ほど / 〜ば〜ほど
meaning: To the extent of / the more X the more Y — degree or proportional correlation
explanation: ほど indicates degree or extent. With a negative it means 'not as X as': 思ったほどじゃない = not as much as I thought. 〜ば〜ほど is the 'the more...the more' pattern expressing proportional increase.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"勉強すればするほど、上手になります。","reading":"べんきょうすればするほど、じょうずになります。","english":"The more you study, the better you get."},{"japanese":"思ったほど難しくなかったです。","reading":"おもったほどむずかしくなかったです。","english":"It wasn't as difficult as I thought."},{"japanese":"命の危険を感じるほどの寒さでした。","reading":"いのちのきけんをかんじるほどのさむさでした。","english":"It was so cold I felt my life was in danger."}]

title: 〜さえ〜ば (if only / as long as)
pattern: N + さえ + V-ば / N + さえあれば
meaning: If only / as long as — marks the single sufficient condition
explanation: さえ〜ば indicates that X is the only thing needed for Y to occur. It highlights the minimum or sole condition. さえ can also mean 'even' when not combined with ば (emphasising an extreme case).
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"お金さえあれば、旅行に行けます。","reading":"おかねさえあれば、りょこうにいけます。","english":"As long as I have money, I can travel."},{"japanese":"健康でさえあれば、それで十分です。","reading":"けんこうでさえあれば、それでじゅうぶんです。","english":"As long as you're healthy, that's enough."},{"japanese":"君さえいれば、他には何もいらない。","reading":"きみさえいれば、ほかにはなにもいらない。","english":"As long as I have you, I need nothing else."}]

title: 〜上に (in addition to / moreover)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + 上に / N の + 上に
meaning: In addition to / on top of that — adds something to an already existing quality
explanation: 上に stacks two qualities or facts, with the second reinforcing or compounding the first. Often both elements are in the same direction (both positive or both negative). More emphatic than だけでなく.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この店は安い上に、サービスもいいです。","reading":"このみせはやすいうえに、サービスもいいです。","english":"This shop is cheap, and on top of that the service is good."},{"japanese":"今日は仕事が忙しい上に、残業まである。","reading":"きょうはしごとがいそがしいうえに、ざんぎょうまである。","english":"Today work is busy, and on top of that there's overtime."},{"japanese":"彼女は美人な上に、頭もいいです。","reading":"かのじょはびじんなうえに、あたまもいいです。","english":"She is beautiful and smart on top of that."}]

title: 〜せいで (because of — negative cause)
pattern: V-た / Adj (plain) + せいで / N の + せいで
meaning: Because of / due to — blames something as the cause of a bad result
explanation: せいで attributes blame for a negative outcome. It is always used for unfavourable results. Contrasts with おかげで (positive results). Can express self-blame or blaming others/circumstances.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"寝坊したせいで、電車に乗り遅れました。","reading":"ねぼうしたせいで、でんしゃにのりおくれました。","english":"Because I overslept, I missed the train."},{"japanese":"雨のせいで、試合が中止になりました。","reading":"あめのせいで、しあいがちゅうしになりました。","english":"Due to the rain, the match was cancelled."},{"japanese":"あなたのせいじゃないですよ。","reading":"あなたのせいじゃないですよ。","english":"It's not your fault."}]

title: 〜おかげで (thanks to — positive cause)
pattern: V-た / Adj (plain) + おかげで / N の + おかげで
meaning: Thanks to / owing to — attributes a positive result to a cause
explanation: おかげで is the positive counterpart of せいで. It credits something (a person, condition, action) for a good outcome. Can be used sarcastically to express an unexpectedly bad result from something supposedly good.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生のおかげで、試験に合格しました。","reading":"せんせいのおかげで、しけんにごうかくしました。","english":"Thanks to my teacher, I passed the exam."},{"japanese":"毎日練習したおかげで、上手になりました。","reading":"まいにちれんしゅうしたおかげで、じょうずになりました。","english":"Thanks to practicing every day, I improved."},{"japanese":"おかげさまで、元気になりました。","reading":"おかげさまで、げんきになりました。","english":"Thanks to everyone, I've recovered."}]

title: 〜ように言う (tell someone to)
pattern: V (plain non-past / ない) + ように言う / 伝える
meaning: Tell / ask someone to do or not do something — indirect command
explanation: ように言う reports an instruction or request given to someone. It softens a command into a request form. Used to relay instructions from one person to another (indirect speech). ように頼む = ask to.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"先生は学生に静かにするように言いました。","reading":"せんせいはがくせいにしずかにするようにいいました。","english":"The teacher told the students to be quiet."},{"japanese":"医者に無理をしないように言われました。","reading":"いしゃにむりをしないようにいわれました。","english":"The doctor told me not to push myself."},{"japanese":"早く来るように伝えてください。","reading":"はやくくるようにつたえてください。","english":"Please tell him to come quickly."}]

title: 〜ものだ (it's natural / used to / should)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + ものだ
meaning: It's natural that / used to / one should — general truths, past habits, or norms
explanation: ものだ has several uses: (1) stating a general truth or natural state of affairs; (2) recalling past habits (〜たものだ = used to); (3) stating what should be done (social norm). Context distinguishes them.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"子供は元気なものだ。","reading":"こどもはげんきなものだ。","english":"Children are naturally energetic."},{"japanese":"子供の頃よく川で遊んだものです。","reading":"こどものころよくかわであそんだものです。","english":"I used to play by the river as a child."},{"japanese":"人に会ったら挨拶するものです。","reading":"ひとにあったらあいさつするものです。","english":"One should greet people when meeting them."}]

title: 〜ものの (although / but)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + ものの
meaning: Although / even though — concession where the expected result doesn't follow
explanation: ものの presents a concession: although the first clause is true, it doesn't lead to the expected result. It carries a nuance of the speaker's dissatisfaction or the unexpected gap between expectation and reality. More formal than が or けど.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語を3年勉強したものの、まだ話せません。","reading":"にほんごを3ねんべんきょうしたものの、まだはなせません。","english":"Although I've studied Japanese for 3 years, I still can't speak it."},{"japanese":"買ったものの、結局使いませんでした。","reading":"かったものの、けっきょくつかいませんでした。","english":"Although I bought it, I ended up not using it."},{"japanese":"行きたいとは思うものの、時間がありません。","reading":"いきたいとはおもうものの、じかんがありません。","english":"Although I want to go, I don't have time."}]

title: 〜ところだ (just about to / just did / in the middle of)
pattern: V (non-past) + ところだ / V-て + いるところだ / V-た + ところだ
meaning: Just about to / in the middle of / just finished — precise timing
explanation: ところ with different verb forms expresses precise timing: non-past + ところ = about to do; ている + ところ = in the middle of doing; た + ところ = just finished. Very useful for explaining current state.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今から出かけるところです。","reading":"いまからでかけるところです。","english":"I'm just about to go out."},{"japanese":"今、ご飯を食べているところです。","reading":"いま、ごはんをたべているところです。","english":"I'm in the middle of eating right now."},{"japanese":"今ちょうど帰ってきたところです。","reading":"いまちょうどかえってきたところです。","english":"I just got home right now."}]

title: 〜場合は (in the case that / if)
pattern: V/Adj (plain) + 場合は / N の + 場合は
meaning: In the case of / if — presents a scenario and describes what applies in that case
explanation: 場合は presents a hypothetical or specific scenario and then states what should be done or what follows. It is more formal than たら and often appears in instructions, manuals, and rules.
